Q1: I tried to organize my study routine around my extracurricular activities, which honestly took up a lot of my time. I'd block out about 24 hours a week for studying, usually in the evenings and weekends. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often set up practice problems or create physical flashcards rather than just reading. My routine was structured but flexible enough to accommodate my other commitments.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was happening that week. I tried to maintain some consistency, but when deadlines approached or my extracurriculars got intense, everything shifted. Some weeks I'd study every day for a few hours, other weeks it would be concentrated bursts. I learned to adapt rather than stress about having a perfect schedule.
Q3: Honestly, I usually logged in when I needed something specific—like checking an assignment deadline or finding a particular resource. I didn't log in just to browse around. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post or a new announcement, and that would prompt me to check in. It was more task-oriented than habit-based for me.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became something I did automatically every day. I'd go through phases where I was more active, especially early in the semester when I was figuring things out. But it didn't stick as a daily routine because I found other ways to study that worked better for my kinesthetic learning style.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually helpful when people were actively engaging with real questions about the material. I also appreciated when there were downloadable resources I could print out and annotate by hand. Videos were useful too, but mainly as a starting point—I'd watch them once and then work through the concepts physically with practice problems or diagrams.
Q6: I often skipped the longer reading materials on the LMS because I found it hard to engage with them on screen. I'd either print them or find alternative explanations elsewhere. Some of the optional quizzes I ignored because they felt repetitive. I also didn't engage much with peer interactions on the platform—only about six times—because I preferred discussing things in person with study groups.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it directly connected to what I'd be tested on or when it helped me actually understand something I was struggling with. If I could apply it practically or if it sparked a real 'aha' moment, that made it valuable. Busywork that felt disconnected from my learning goals? I'd skip it without guilt.
Q8: Required activities carried more weight in my mind because they affected my grade, so I prioritized those even if they weren't the most engaging. Optional activities felt like extras I'd get to if I had time, which I often didn't. Sometimes the optional stuff was actually more interesting, but the fear of missing required deadlines always won out in my planning.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, print them out, and work through them with highlighters and handwritten notes. I also used textbooks and external YouTube videos to supplement what was on the LMS. My kinesthetic style means I need to physically interact with materials, which the LMS doesn't really capture.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours working through problem sets by hand or meeting with a study group in the library, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd be deeply engaged—writing, drawing diagrams, explaining concepts to friends—but my LMS activity would look completely dead. It was frustrating because it didn't reflect my actual effort.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video or document once, download it or take screenshots, and then never touch the LMS version again. I preferred having everything on my laptop or printed so I could study anywhere without needing internet. Once I had what I needed, the LMS became irrelevant for that particular resource.
Q12: There were moments when I felt genuinely scared about whether I was keeping up, especially mid-semester when assignments piled up and my completion rate was lower than I wanted. My extracurriculars were demanding, and sometimes I'd feel overwhelmed trying to balance everything. Those were the times when logging into the LMS felt like facing a mountain I wasn't sure I could climb.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued from extracurriculars or had competing deadlines, the LMS was the first thing I'd cut corners on. I'd prioritize the essential tasks and let the optional engagement slide. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections on the LMS and seek help elsewhere instead. It wasn't ideal, but it was survival mode.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open a video or reading and realize my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be going through the motions—scrolling, clicking—but not actually absorbing anything. Those sessions were frustrating because I knew I was wasting time, but I was too tired or stressed to engage properly. I'd usually have to come back later when I could focus.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even when I knew they were important. Sometimes it was because I was scared of not doing well, so I'd avoid starting until the pressure became unbearable. Other times, my extracurriculars genuinely took priority. The mindfulness exercises I learned helped me recognize this pattern and push through the fear, but it was a struggle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. I'd ramp up my LMS activity significantly as deadlines approached, cramming in videos and resources I'd been putting off. Without a deadline, I had very little motivation to engage with the platform. It's not the healthiest approach, but the structure of due dates kept me accountable when nothing else did.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I received constructive comments, I felt motivated to improve and would revisit materials to understand where I went wrong. But when feedback was delayed or generic, it felt discouraging. Good feedback made me feel like my effort mattered; poor feedback made me question why I was trying so hard.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to work within, which I appreciated. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my 24 study hours each week. However, the structure also felt rigid at times—it didn't always accommodate my learning style or my need for flexibility around extracurriculars. I worked within it, but I often wished for more adaptability.
Q19: The course design made engagement harder when activities were purely reading-based or required long screen time. I'm kinesthetic, so I need to do things with my hands to learn. When there were interactive elements or practical applications, engagement was much easier. The discussion forums were good in theory, but participation felt forced sometimes rather than organic.
Q20: I think students might be searching for something—trying to understand the material, looking for resources that click with their learning style, or feeling anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually producing work. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity. Sometimes it's just students trying to figure out what they're supposed to do or feeling overwhelmed.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was low, but I was studying extensively offline—printing materials, using textbooks, working in study groups. I passed the course and did reasonably well on exams because my learning happened outside the platform. Low LMS activity doesn't mean low effort or low understanding; it might just mean different study methods.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when students are active, what they click on, how long they spend on certain pages. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and offline learning. They can't capture whether someone is truly understanding the material or just clicking through. They also miss emotional states, like when a student is scared or overwhelmed but still pushing forward.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively wrestling with the material, asking questions, making connections, and genuinely trying to understand rather than just memorize. It's about mental investment, not just physical presence. For me, engagement often looks like scribbling notes, discussing concepts with peers, or practicing problems repeatedly until they make sense—things that don't show up in any log.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my resilience—how I pushed through fear and stress to keep going even when things felt overwhelming. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices that helped me manage my anxiety, or the hours I spent in study groups learning collaboratively. It also wouldn't show how my extracurriculars shaped my time management or how being a kinesthetic learner influenced everything I did outside the platform.
